**Article Title: Mapping Transboundary Evapotranspiration: Methodological Implications for Water Management in the Milk River Basin**

**Author Names:** David Ketchum1\*, Blake Minor2, Charles Morton2, Christian Dunkerly2, Christopher Spence3, Roy Sando4

**Affiliations:** 1 Montana Climate Office, W.A. Franke College of Forestry and Conservation, University of Montana, Missoula, Montana 59812, 2 Division of Hydrologic Sciences, Western Regional Climate Center, Desert Research Institute, Reno, Nevada 89512, 3 Environment and Climate Change Canada, Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, S7N 3H5, 4 U.S. Geological Survey Wyoming-Montana Water Science Center, Helena, Montana 59601

**\* Corresponding Author:** david.ketchum@mso.umt.edu.  
**Abstract**

Landsat-based evapotranspiration (ET) models are widely applied within the conterminous United States, yet their extension across international boundaries is hindered by the political limits of commonly used gridded meteorological datasets. In the transboundary St. Mary and Milk River watersheds (SMMW), where a 1921 International Joint Commission Order requires ongoing water apportionment between the U.S. and Canada, spatially resolved ET data remain a critical gap. We applied the Operational Simplified Surface Energy Balance (SSEBop) model with Landsat imagery and North American Land Data Assimilation System Phase 2 (NLDAS-2) meteorological forcing to produce a 39-year (1985-2023), 30 m resolution, monthly actual ET (ETa) dataset spanning the international border. A Monte Carlo variance decomposition of NLDAS-2 reference ET (ETo) errors at 48 agricultural weather stations showed that vapor pressure deficit dominates ETo error (68%), followed by mean temperature (17%), wind speed (11%), and net radiation (3%). We developed spatially continuous monthly bias-correction surfaces using inverse distance weighting, reducing ETo RMSE by over 20% (0.83 to 0.66 mm day⁻¹). Despite documented latitudinal precipitation discontinuities in NLDAS-2 at the Canada-U.S. border, we found no significant cross-border bias in ETo-controlling variables. Validation against eight eddy covariance stations yielded monthly ETa RMSE of 23.86 mm month⁻¹ (r² \= 0.65) and daily overpass ETa RMSE of 1.16 mm day⁻¹ (r² \= 0.56). Error decomposition attributed approximately 60% of ETa error to the remote sensing component and 40% to meteorological forcing. This study provides a reproducible framework for harmonizing international reanalysis data in Landsat-based ET applications and delivers the first spatially continuous ET dataset for the SMMW.

**Keywords: evapotranspiration; remote sensing; Landsat; bias correction; reanalysis; transboundary water resources; SSEBop.**

1. **Introduction**

Timely and accurate estimates of water use are critical in management of transboundary water resources. Efficient collection and provision of spatially and temporally resolved water resources information enables the prevention and resolution of water distribution disputes, protection of the environment, advanced scientific research, and policy development and implementation. There has been a continuous effort to measure and monitor water resources in the transboundary Saint Mary and Milk River watersheds (SMMW) since the 1921 *Order by the International Joint Commission*, which established specific criteria allowing for the apportionment of the rivers’ water between Canadian and American use (Halliday and Faveri, 2007). The details of the Order require the computation of the rivers’ natural flow, and thus necessitate estimates of diversions to, and return flows from, municipal, industrial, and agricultural water users. In practice, this necessitates the estimate of consumptive use, the loss of water irretrievably to the atmosphere (Allen et al., 2007). By far the largest consumptive use in the SMMW is crop water use via evapotranspiration (ET) from irrigated lands.

The economic importance of irrigated agriculture and potential impacts of heavy use make ET among the most important water fluxes to understand in agricultural regions worldwide (Foley et al. 2011). In the SMMW, irrigation supports a major agricultural industry that generates over USD $1 billion and CAN $7 billion annually in farm income in the U.S. and Canada portions of the basins, areas served by irrigation canals sourced from the SMMW, and immediate surroundings (National Agricultural Statistics Service, 2022; Statistics Canada, 2021). Irrigated operations represent a large fraction of farm activity and cover an extensive area of approximately 400 km2 and 1600 km2 in the U.S. and Canadian areas of the watershed, respectively (International Joint Commission, 2008). Despite being essential for accurately quantifying water use, ET data still represent a significant gap in transboundary water information. While the other major water balance components (i.e., precipitation and streamflow) are routinely measured, station-based estimates of actual ET (ETa) through lysimetry, eddy covariance, and indirect methods are expensive and difficult, resulting in a low density of observations. 

The scarcity of ground-based ETa observations and the dependence of ETa on spatially and temporally variable vegetation, soil, and meteorological conditions makes the spatial interpolation of ETa unreliable. The application of sophisticated algorithms using satellite remote sensing (SRS) and gridded meteorology data have vastly improved the feasibility of quantifying the heterogeneous and dynamic signature of ETa over the landscape (Wulder et al. 2022). The Landsat mission has provided a record of high-resolution multispectral images taken from polar orbiting satellites with revisit times of 8 to 16 days and has maintained temporal and instrumental continuity through the deployment of a series of instruments since the late 1970s. The 30 meter (m) resolution of the optical bands and 100 m resolution of the thermal bands, and frequent revisit frequency allow for a spatial and temporal resolution adequate to capture the phenology of crops at the individual field scale (Wulder et al. 2022). The recent implementation of automated SRS-based ETa algorithms that exploit the massively parallelized computational architecture of cloud-based services such as Google Earth Engine has accelerated adoption and acceptance in management contexts of these ETa estimation approaches (Gorelick et al. 2017; Melton et al. 2022; Pearson et al. 2024; Huntington et al. 2025). 

The Operational Simplified Surface Energy Balance model (SSEBop; Senay et al. 2013\) has been widely applied using Landsat imagery over the conterminous United States (CONUS) and at regional scales for investigation of a broad range of water-resources and ecological questions (Poon and Kinoshita, 2018; Senay et al. 2019, 2022; Lurtz et al. 2020; Ketchum et al. 2024). While the polar-orbiting Landsat missions operate in a consistent manner independent of location and political geography, the gridded data commonly in use to drive the meteorological component (i.e., reference ET) of SSEBop applications is often constrained to political bounds, as is the case with popular gridded meteorological datasets in operational use in remote sensing estimates of ETa within the US. Using a fully parameterized gridded dataset such as the daily, 4 km resolution gridded meteorology dataset gridMET (Daly et al. 2008; Abatzoglou 2013\) would provide the benefit of high-frequency and low-latency gridded meteorology information, however, the coverage of gridMET is limited to the contiguous United States and would not cover the entirety of SMMW. One benefit of such fully parameterized gridded datasets is that they offer high-frequency estimates of all drivers of atmospheric demand, enabling the calculation of standard reference ET using methods like the American Society of Civil Engineers’ Standardized Penman-Monteith grass reference ET (ETo; ASCE, 2005).

International gridded climate reanalysis datasets offer a similar suite of variables; however, to our knowledge, they have not yet been applied over international boundaries in Landsat-based ETa approaches. While bias and error in gridded meteorology and climate reanalysis data in agricultural contexts in the U.S. have been identified (Blankenau et al. 2020), those associated with their application to high-resolution ETa estimates over international extents have not been analyzed. One such international climate reanalysis product is the North American Land Data Assimilation System, Phase 2 (NLDAS-2), a land surface modeling system that provides comprehensive information about land surface conditions and fluxes over North America (23°N to 53°N, 67°W to 125°W; Xia et al. 2012a, 2012b). Given the interest in the accuracy and uncertainty of transboundary water-resources information, the need for systematic, unbiased international applications of SRS-based ETa estimates is evident. Apparent latitudinal precipitation anomalies over the Canada-U.S. border have been identified in NLDAS-2 (Xu et al. 2019). However, it is not well understood if the potentially disparate meteorological monitoring networks across international boundaries create bias in the gridded estimates of atmospheric demand, and thus whether they impact the accuracy of SRS-based ETa estimates.

The core modeling approach used by SSEBop and many other ETa modeling approaches is the formulation of ETa as the product of separate crop state and meteorology components (Blaney and Criddle, 1962; Jensen et al. 1971; Allen et al. 1998). Because ETa is computed as the product of a satellite-derived crop coefficient (ETf) and meteorologically derived reference ET (ETo), any systematic bias in ETo propagates proportionally into ETa; a 10% overestimate in ETo produces a corresponding 10% overestimate in ETa for a given ETf. This multiplicative coupling makes the accuracy and bias structure of the gridded meteorological forcing a first-order control on the accuracy of the final ETa product, and motivates the detailed ETo error analysis and bias correction undertaken in this study. Several efforts have assessed the sensitivity and error of SRS-based models (Choragudi, 2011; Chen et al. 2016; Sepúlveda, 2021), and the sensitivity and error of ETo models (Beven et al. 1979; Estévez et al. 2009), yet to our knowledge the contribution of error by reanalysis meteorology data, and the relative error of the crop state and meteorological components of SSEBop have not been analyzed.

In this study, we apply the Landsat-based Google Earth Engine implementation of the SSEBop ETa model using NLDAS-2 meteorological forcing data over the SMMW. We perform an error analysis on the NLDAS-2 forcing data and compare NLDAS-2 accuracy in the United States and Canada. We also compare NLDAS-2 with gridMET. We use agricultural weather station data to perform a one-at-a-time error analysis and to estimate systematic bias associated with NLDAS-2-based estimates of ETo. We perform a regional optimization of the NLDAS-2-derived meteorological forcing at 48 surface meteorology stations located in agricultural contexts in the U.S. and Canada and create bias correction surfaces on a monthly basis. We analyze the accuracy of daily and monthly estimates of ETa from SSEBop at eight EC stations surrounding the SMMW. This study answers the acute need for ETa data that are developed in a systematic and unbiased manner that can be applied using international datasets in areas of critical transboundary water resource investigations. Beyond quantifying water use, this study aims to demonstrate a scalable geostatistical approach for harmonizing disparate international meteorological datasets, addressing a common barrier in transboundary remote sensing applications.

2. **Materials and Methods**  
   

**2.1 Study Area**

We focus on the transboundary watersheds of the Saint Mary (St. Mary) and Milk Rivers, both of which rise in the mountains and foothills of central Montana. The St. Mary runs from the mountains of Glacier National Park approximately 180 km to its confluence with the Old Man River near Lethbridge, Alberta, draining an area of 3,800 km2 (Figure 1). The Milk River flows nearly 1,200 km from its source near Browning, Montana north into Alberta, and returns to the U.S. before reaching its mouth at the confluence with the Missouri River near Fort Peck, Montana, draining an area of nearly 60,000 km2, including areas of Montana, Alberta, and Saskatchewan. While the two river systems are naturally non-contributing, the systems have been linked hydrologically since the completion of the St. Mary Canal in 1915, which diverts water from the St. Mary River through a system of canals and siphons to the North Fork of the Milk River, where it supplements irrigation internationally. Since 2000 the canal has diverted an average of 215 million m3 yr\-1 from the St. Mary to the Milk River basin (Bureau of Reclamation, 2024). The combined system is semi-arid, with a maximum mean (1994 \- 2023\) annual precipitation at the headwaters of the St. Mary River of nearly 800 mm year\-1 to a minimum of around 270 mm year\-1 in the lower basin and receives a mean basin-wide precipitation of about 350 mm year\-1 (Abatzoglou et al. 2018).

**![][image1]**  
**Figure 1\.** The Milk-St Mary watersheds. In (A), the study area is shown, where the St. Mary USGS Hydrologic Unit Code-4 is outlined, and where the Hydrologic Unit Code-8 outlines the Milk River watershed (Jones et al. 2022). Flux towers and weather stations are displayed. Land cover is from the North American Land Change Monitoring System, which is remapped to six generalized land cover classes (Commission for Environmental Cooperation, 2024). In (B) the Landsat World Reference System-2 scene footprints used to select Landsat 5, 7, 8, and 9 data according to period-specific sensor availability are shown over study watersheds.  
 

**2.2 Weather Station Data**

Data were sourced from High Plains Regional Climate Center (HPRCC, 2019), Alberta Climate Information System (ACIS, 2023), United States Bureau of Reclamation AgriMet program (Bureau of Reclamation, 2023), Natural Resources Conservation Service Soil Climate Analysis Network (NRCS, 2023), and Environment Climate Change Canada (ECCC, 2023). Forty-eight stations (Figure 1\) were selected after reviewing locations for siting issues and performing quality assurance and control (QAQC; Supplement 1). QAQC was performed before processing ETo calculations using Agweather-qaqc (Dunkerly et al. 2024), an open-source Python software, following Allen (1996, 2008), ASCE Environmental and Water Resources Institute, and United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO-56) guidelines. To facilitate comparison with gridded data, we followed FAO-56 guidelines to compute the inputs to the ASCE ETo equation: mean daily temperature (Tmean; C), net radiation (Rn; MJ m-2 day-1), vapor pressure deficit (VPD; kPa), and 2 m height wind speed (m sec-1) from available observation parameters.

**2.3 NLDAS-2 Data and Analysis**

Gridded climate forcing data were derived from NLDAS-2, a near real-time (\~4-day lag), 1/8th\-degree, hourly gridded land surface and meteorological forcing model over North America, from N 25° to N 53°, available 1979 to present. NLDAS-2 does not explicitly account for irrigation and associated ET-induced changes to near-surface atmospheric boundary layer coupling processes (Ozdogan and Rodell, 2010). In arid and semi-arid environments characterized by high advection and limited water availability, field-scale land surface-atmospheric feedbacks have been well documented (Allen et al. 1983; Temesgen et al. 1999; Szilagyi and Schepers, 2014). NLDAS-2 may not accurately estimate ETo for irrigated areas, necessitating the characterization of NLDAS-2 error and evaluation of systematic biases prior to application of NLDAS-2 derived ETo as a forcing in the SSEBop model. 

We calculated Tmean, Rn, VPD, and wind speed for each NLDAS-2 grid cell intersecting the weather stations and found differences with observed values, assuming error-free observations. We estimated the parameters of the residual distribution (observations minus NLDAS-2 estimates) and correlation matrix for the variable estimates and residuals for each station. To quantify the relative variance contribution of each meteorological input to ETo error, we utilized a Monte Carlo simulation with an empirical copula sampling approach (Sklar, 1959; see Supplement for detailed methodology and equations).  
We further analyzed systematic bias at the monthly and annual time step of NLDAS-2 ETo, calculating the difference in ETo for each complete month in the weather station record and the overall mean monthly and annual bias at each station. Monthly station bias correction factors were interpolated throughout the Milk River Basin study area using an inverse distance weighting (IDW) approach with a continuous, 1/8th\-degree resolution raster with a maximum of 25 stations. We applied the ETo bias correction surface to daily NLDAS-2 derived ETo for respective months to create a spatially explicit, bias-corrected reference ET product that is representative of the local areas and conditions within the study area (see further explanation in Supplement). The bias correction surfaces were subsequently spatially resampled and compared with bias correction surfaces made by the OpenET Project in the United States using gridMET meteorological data (Melton et al. 2022).

**2.4 SSEBop Model**

The SSEBop model was applied over the study in and around the SMMW with Landsat satellite images and ancillary gridded data inputs hosted on Google Earth Engine (Gorelick et al. 2017). SSEBop uses a satellite psychrometric approach to estimate the difference between observed Landsat thermal infrared-based surface temperature and a wet-bulb reference surface temperature to estimate ETf, the ratio of actual ET (ETa) to alfalfa reference ET (ETr; the rate of ET of a vigorous, 0.5 m alfalfa reference crop; Senay et al. 2013; ASCE, 2005). ETa is subsequently estimated by finding the product of the meteorologically derived ETr and ETf on Landsat-capture dates over quality-masked images.

ETa \= ETr \* ETf		(4)

To produce a daily time series of SSEBop ETa estimates, ETf is calculated for all clear pixels on each Landsat overpass date. At each pixel location, ETf estimates on dates without Landsat overpass data are linearly interpolated between capture dates and Eqn 4 is applied daily. SSEBop is parameterized using the surface psychrometric constant *ᵞs* \[K\-1\], the Landsat-derived dry-bulb surface temperature *Ts* \[K\], and the wet-bulb surface temperature, *Tc* \[K\]:

 ETf \=1 \-ᵞs(Ts \- Tc) 		(5)

The parameter ᵞs is derived using estimates of net radiation and actual vapor pressure during the Landsat overpass time and is described in detail in Senay and others (2013); Senay (2018). We utilized the Forcing and Normalizing Operation (FANO) parameterization of SSEBop to resolve the idealized wet-bulb surface temperature (Tc\*; Senay et al. 2023). This approach allows for the estimation of Tc\* for any given Landsat pixel based on predefined temperature differences (dT) and observations of NDVI and surface temperature (Ts) (see Supplement 4 for detailed formulation). The bias-corrected NLDAS-2 derived ETo described in the previous section was used to support the calculation of daily ET between satellite acquisitions, which occur every 8 days over each scene footprint when combining imagery from two satellites (e.g., Landsat 7 with Landsat 9, or Landsat 5 with Landsat 8), or every 16 using a single satellite. Due to the sun-synchronous orbit of the Landsat satellites, scene footprints increasingly overlap poleward, providing further image captures in high latitudes. Smoke and clouds were masked from the Landsat images using the included quality image band. Landsat images exceeding 70% cloud cover were excluded from the analysis. 

We used open-source Python software packages *openet-core and openet-ssebop* developed by OpenET (Melton et al. 2022\) to analyze about 33,500 usable study area images in Google Earth Engine, over the 39-year study period, providing a mean of 16.5 and 21 clear pixel views per year over the St. Mary and Milk River watersheds, respectively. We exported 468 monthly ETa rasters at the native Landsat resolution (30 m) and summarized the data over different land cover types obtained for the U.S and Canada, both of which are available at 30 m resolution (Boryan et al. 2011; Fisette et al. 2013). Land-cover masks were generated from harmonized USDA Cropland Data Layer (CDL; Boryan et al., 2011\) and AAFC Annual Crop Inventory (ACI; Fisette et al., 2013\) products remapped to a simplified six-class land cover layer (Forest, Grass/Shrubland, Cropland, Alpine/Barren, Developed, Surface Water), from which the Forest, Grass/Shrubland, and Cropland classes were used as masks to calculate annual SSEBop ETa volumes. For years before 2009, 2009 land-cover masks were applied; for years after 2022, 2022 masks were applied. 

**2.5 Eddy Covariance Validation**

We compared SSEBop daily and monthly ETa estimates to ground-based data collected at eddy covariance (EC) flux tower systems in and around the study area. The *in situ* micrometeorological data and energy balance station data include wind speed, humidity, vapor pressure, air temperature, shortwave radiation, net radiation, sensible heat flux, latent heat flux, and/or soil heat flux. We used data from 13 EC towers, finding eight had enough valid data for use in validation, including two that were overseen by ECCC (Sando et al., 2026\) and six that are part of the AmeriFlux network (Novick et al. 2018). ECCC data underwent standard corrections including coordinate rotation, WPL adjustment, and energy balance closure  following standard micrometeorological methods (Kaimal and Finnigan, 1994; Webb et al. 1980; Horst, 1997; Massman, 2000; see Supplement 5 for details). AmeriFlux EC data were processed and reviewed using project QAQC workflows (Supplement 5). Station data records varied in length from 2 months to 9 years, with an average of 2 years of quality resampled daily data per eddy covariance station. ETa estimates were sampled in upwind footprints at each of the EC flux towers, where multiple Landsat pixel-aligned flux footprints (i.e., 3x3, 5x5, and 7x7) were tested for SSEBop ETa \-observation correlation, after Melton and others (2022) and Volk and others (2024; see example footprint in Figure S1). Flux site data was not used in the ETo bias correction workflow.

We repeated our Monte Carlo approach to quantifying error through the decomposition of variance using daily SSEBop data and EC observations. We chose to focus on overpass date SSEBop estimates to simplify our decomposition of relative error contributions to ETa by the ETf and ETo components of the model. This approach provides an estimate of the relative contribution to error by remote sensing-based (ETf) and meteorology-based (ETo) components of the algorithm. SSEBop overpass-date ETa estimates are temporally discontinuous, occurring at a sub-daily frequency , therefore we did not attempt to mimic the temporal autocorrelation structure in the data, and simplified sampling by using the unbounded Johnson’s Su distribution where the EC station of interest had greater than 20 overpass-date observation-model estimate pairs (Johnson, 1949). Where stations had fewer than 20 observation-model estimate pairs, we simply sampled the observed residuals randomly. As in the ETo error analysis, we used Eqn. 4 to estimate ETa, having perturbed either the ETo or ETf variables with 1,000 draws of synthetic errors each, and in the same manner as described above, determined the relative contribution to error of the two inputs. While long-term NLDAS-2 *dT* climatology is used internally in this SSEBop implementation, the *dT* parameter was not perturbed, and was instead treated as an independent component of SSEBop for simplicity in variance decomposition. 

    **3.0 Results**

**3.1 NLDAS-2 Error Analysis and ETo Bias Correction**

Approximately 180,000 meteorological observations obtained at weather stations were matched with NLDAS-2 estimates of ETo. Comparison of these data showed NLDAS-2 underestimated ETo by 0.01 mm day\-1 on average, with a slightly skewed error distribution in which the NLDAS-2 estimates of ETo exceeding that of the observation by more than 2 mm day\-1 were slightly more numerous than underestimates of the same magnitude (Figure 2). We found the ETo residuals (i.e., observed minus NLDAS-2) were correlated with the input meteorological residuals, with the highest Pearson correlation between ETo and vapor pressure deficit (VPD; r \= 0.61), followed by mean air temperature (Tmean; r \= 0.57), net radiation and wind each with r \= 0.43 (Figure 2C). Overall, the errors are seasonally compensatory; NLDAS-2 underestimates Rn while overestimating the other positively related controls on ETo (i.e., VPD, Tmean, and wind speed), with the balance shifting between winter and summer as detailed below. 

![][image2]

**Figure 2\.** NLDAS-2 ETo error characterization across 180,051 matched station-day observations at 48 weather stations. (A) Distribution of daily ETo residuals (observation minus NLDAS-2); the red curve is a kernel density estimate. (B) Distribution of per-station mean monthly ETo residuals shown as boxplots for each month; the box spans the interquartile range (25th to 75th percentile), the horizontal bar shows the median, whiskers extend to 1.5× IQR, and dots show outliers; the red line shows the daily median ETo residual. (C) Pearson correlations between NLDAS-2 input variable estimates (upper triangle) and their residuals with respect to observations (lower triangle), for net radiation (Rn), mean temperature (Tmean), vapor pressure deficit (VPD), wind speed (Wind), and reference ET (ETo). 

Mean monthly residuals show a consistent seasonal structure (Figure 2B): NLDAS-2 underestimates ETo during winter (December–February), producing positive residuals at all 48 stations, and overestimates ETo during summer, with 45 (July) and 44 (August) of the 48 stations showing a mean monthly overestimate of ETo (Figure S1). This time-dependent bias supports monthly, rather than daily, correction. A positive bias in NLDAS-2 derived ETo relative to station ETo was found at 34 stations for the growing season (Apr-Oct), and 30 stations for the calendar year (Figure 2B). NLDAS-2 estimates in winter are characterized by underestimates of Rn (mean underestimate of 0.18 MJ day\-1) and overestimates of mean daily temperature (0.79 C), while summer estimates are characterized by comparably large overestimates of VPD (0.55 kPa), Tmean (2.6 C), and wind (0.77 m sec\-1). These notable seasonal errors lead to a mean ETo overestimate of 0.52 mm day\-1 in the summer and an underestimate of 0.4 mm day\-1 in the winter.

![][image3]

**Figure 3\.** Relationship between NLDAS-2 ETo residuals and residuals in each meteorological input variable, and the Monte Carlo variance decomposition results. Panels (a–d) show hex-bin density plots of residuals of vapor pressure deficit (VPD; kPa), net radiation (Rn; MJ m2 day\-1), wind speed (m s1), and mean daily temperature (Mean Temp; °C) plotted against ETo residuals (mm day1); n \= 180,051 for all panels and the r² between each variable residual and ETo residual is shown.  

NLDAS-2 meteorological input residuals have similar distributions in the U.S. and Canada portions of the study area; VPD, Rn, and Tmean have very similar mean, spread, and shape, their centers differing by 0.03 kPa, 0.27 MJ m2 day\-1, and 1.1 C, respectively (Figure S1). The exception is the wind speed residuals; while the distribution is similar in shape and spread, estimates are slightly more accurate at the weather stations in Canada than at those in the US, with mean errors (both overestimates by NLDAS-2) of 0.12 m sec\-1 and 0.81 m sec\-1, respectively. In terms of ETo estimates, the station \- NLDAS-2 ETo correlation is similar in the U.S. and Canada (r2 \= 0.87 and 0.89, respectively), and both have very slight bias (0.10 and \-0.13 mm day\-1), however the slope of the station \- NLDAS-2 regression in the U.S. is high (1.03); the overestimate of ETo by NLDAS-2 increases as ETo rates climb.

When compared with gridMET, NLDAS-2 generally shows a larger standard deviation in residuals, with the exception of wind speed, where NLDAS-2 had a slightly lower standard deviation (Figure S4). gridMET is more accurate among all ETo input variables and shows an especially large improvement over NLDAS-2 in its estimate of Tmean, where its bias is lower by over 1.32 C overall. Bias in gridMET is in the same direction as NLDAS-2 for all variables. Despite the error and variance among ETo input variables in NLDAS-2, the mean estimate over the 37k matched observation-model estimate pairs at the 18 stations in the U.S. was identical to that of gridMET (mean ETo residual \= 0.13 mm day\-1). Spatial comparisons of ETo estimates show generally higher estimates by gridMET in the more arid central and eastern regions of the study area, while NLDAS-2 makes much higher estimates (often more than 200 mm or more) in the mountainous terrain in the western region of the study area (Figure S5). 

We found that NLDAS-2 errors had notable cross-correlation, with residual correlations ranging from r \= 0.58 between Tmean and VPD to r \= 0.03 between Rn and VPD (Figure 2C). Overall, the relative strength of correlation between input variables and ETo was matched in terms of residual correlations. The variance decomposition shows that VPD dominates ETo error (68%), with wind speed and mean temperature errors making smaller contributions (11% and 17% respectively), while net radiation error plays a minor role (3%; Figure S9).

Summertime bias correction surfaces show ETo ratios are generally low, indicating a positive bias in NLDAS-2 ETo for a majority of the SMMW during peak growing season months (Figure S9). Average ETo ratios from our bias-correction analysis vary across the study area with greater ratios (i.e., negative bias in NLDAS-2 ETo) observed along mountain front areas in the St. Mary basin and the western portion of the Milk River basin, and smaller ratios (i.e., positive bias in NLDAS-2 ETo) observed in the eastern portion of the Milk River basin. The application of the bias correction factors lowered the RMSE of ETo estimates at the weather stations by about 20%, from 0.83 mm day\-1 to 0.66 mm day\-1 (Figure S7). Bias correction at flux tower sites was generally successful, with the notable exception of the grassland site US-FPe, which showed a positive bias of around 20%.

![][image4]

**Figure 4\.** Comparison of SSEBop ETa estimates and flux tower observations at eight stations in the study area, differentiated by color and marker. (A) Daily overpass-date comparisons (n \= 680; RMSE \= 1.16 mm day1, r² \= 0.56, slope \= 0.95). (B) Monthly comparisons (n \= 144; RMSE \= 23.86 mm month1, r² \= 0.65, slope \= 1.05). The 1:1 line (black dashed) and regression statistics are shown in each panel.

**3.2 SSEBop ETa** 

The 3x3 flux footprint had the highest accuracy across all sites with complete monthly comparisons with a root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 23.86 mm month\-1, coefficient of determination (r2) of 0.65, and a slope of 1.05 (Figure 4B). Out of eight total sites used for validation (Table S1), six were croplands with a mean of 5.8 monthly observations and two were grasslands with an average of 90 monthly observations. Whereas the average RMSE, r2, and slope for cropland sites were 31.81 mm month\-1, 0.85, and 1.03, respectively, the average RMSE, r2, and slope for grassland sites were 16.82 mm month\-1, 0.83, and 0.7, respectively. Daily overpass-date comparison for 680 days showed RMSE of 1.16 mm day⁻¹, r² of 0.56, and a slope of 0.95.  Per-site validation metrics for all eddy covariance stations are provided in Supplement Table S1.

Additional validation was completed by comparing the average growing season NLDAS-2 ETo\-based SSEBop ETa estimates from the current study to OpenET average growing season gridMET ETo\-based SSEBop ETa estimates (Melton et al. 2022\) for the U.S. portion of the study area. Patterns in the spatial distribution of the differences in ETa within the study (Figure S8) are similar to patterns observed in the ETo difference map due to the direct use of ETo data in the production of the ETa estimates; in non-agricultural and mountainous regions, we see larger differences between the two ETa estimates. When comparing the NLDAS-2 ETo\-based SSEBop ETa estimates to the OpenET gridMET ETo\-based SSEBop ETa estimates at eddy covariance flux towers we see that NLDAS-2 ETo\-based SSEBop ETa estimates are slightly higher than the OpenET gridMET ETo\-based SSEBop ETa estimates, but overall, they are in general agreement. 

Error decomposition of the ETo and ETf components found about 60% of error was derived from the remote sensing-based component of the SSEBop algorithm. Analysis of monthly ETa over the study area by land use type shows that the forested landscape, while accounting for the smallest area in the study watersheds (1,535 km2), represents the highest mean ETa rate, at about 717 mm yr\-1 (Figure 5). The grassland/shrubland land class is the largest by extent at 41,500 km2 and has the lowest mean ETa at about 296 mm yr\-1. Cropland covers about 18,300 km2 in the combined watersheds and has a mean ETa of about 333 mm yr\-1. These mean ETa values over each land cover class result in mean annual volumetric ETa of approximately 1.1 km³ for forest, 6.1 km³ for cropland, and 12.3 km³ for grass/shrubland, in the St. Mary and Milk River watersheds. We found a slight increase in mean annual ETa rates during the study period; when the first and second halves of the study period (i.e., 1985-2003 and 2004-2023) are compared, we note ETa rates increasing by 15 mm yr\-1, 13 mm yr\-1, and 22 mm yr\-1 in croplands, grass/shrubland, and forests, respectively.  
![][image5]

**Figure 5\.** The monthly ETa of the three major land use types in the region is shown as a time series (A). The mean annual ETa (km3) is shown in the St. Mary and Milk River basins, with an inset illustrating the high resolution of the data, where individual center pivot-irrigated fields may be resolved (B).

    **4.0 Discussion**

Our analysis revealed that NLDAS-2 bias is not spatially random but exhibits distinct regional patterns—underestimating aridity in the U.S. plains while performing better in Canadian grasslands. By modeling these error surfaces explicitly, rather than applying a single basin-wide adjustment, we preserved local meteorological heterogeneity while correcting the broad-scale systematic drift. This highlights the necessity of spatially explicit bias correction (e.g., IDW or Kriging) over simple linear shifting when downscaling coarse reanalysis data for high-resolution (30 m) ET mapping.

Overall agreement in the 180k matched observation-NLDAS-2 estimates of ETo over the study area, where the mean NLDAS-2 underestimates by 0.01 mm day\-1, suggests NLDAS-2 is a good source of data to drive the meteorological component of the SSEBop model (Figure 2A). The spread (i.e., low r2 values) describing the relation between ETo error and the input meteorological variable error imply that there is a substantial random error associated with each input variable (Figure 3). Despite the noisy relationships, the correlations portray weak linear relations among input variable errors. These error correlations, however weak, imply a systematic relation at least between errors of VPD, Rn, and Tmean and ETo, and that this bias can be systematically corrected. Further, the clear pattern of wintertime underestimates (and summertime overestimates) by NLDAS-2 of ETo at the 48 study area weather stations imply such a systematic error is highly time dependent, and that the error may be systematically corrected on a monthly basis (Figure 2B). Conversely, the noise in the daily median ETo residual has little time-dependent structure at short time scales and suggests a daily correction is unlikely to increase ETo estimate accuracy without further information regarding the source of error at the daily frequency. We speculate these errors are related to low model resolution and model underspecification, including the lack of constraint on water use by ETa, and are likely associated with differences in upwind land use, soil moisture, irrigation status, and vegetation type. Flux sites were not used in the bias correction surface calculation, and thus provide a useful validation of the bias correction surface and insight into the limitation of the approach; correcting ETo using meteorological stations sited in agricultural landscapes may leave non-agricultural ETa with substantial bias. This implies the approach should be tailored to the land use type of interest for the study.

Comparison of NLDAS-2 errors in the U.S. and Canada show very similar distributions, with both the means and variances among the two country’s NLDAS-2 estimates of VPD, Rn, and Tmean of very similar magnitude, with wind speed the exception, showing a difference of mean wind speed error of about 0.7 m sec\-1 (Figure S1). This implies that ETo-controlling meteorological data assimilation by the NLDAS-2 ingestion system is largely unbiased, and experiences little systematic difference in weather observations used in supplying observation data. This is a somewhat unexpected result given documented latitudinal precipitation anomalies over the Canada-US border in NLDAS-2 (Xu et al. 2019). The higher slope of the observation-NLDAS-2 ETo scatter implies that NLDAS-2 captures aridity effects well in Canada, while overestimating aridity in the U.S. This is likely explained by overestimates of NLDAS-2 wind in the U.S.

Blankenau and others (2020) found that gridded meteorological datasets generally overestimate air temperature, radiation, and wind speed, and underestimate humidity in agricultural land covers, a finding confirmed in our results of analysis of both gridMET and NLDAS-2. NLDAS-2 estimates of ETo input variables show lower accuracy than those of gridMET in the U.S., likely explained by gridMET’s higher accuracy in estimates of Tmean (Figure S4). The narrow error distributions in gridMET present a more easily corrected systematic error as seen in comparison of ETo model-to-observation correlations (gridMET r2 \= 0.91, NLDAS-2 r2 \= 0.88), and thus could be more successfully bias-corrected with a systematic shift in the ETo estimate. In the case of NLDAS-2, while the correlation between model and station-observed ETo is high, the slope diverges to a greater degree, such that a correction of the data through a simple shift would be expected to result in less accurate results. The generally higher accuracy of gridMET in its estimates of input meteorological variables is likely explained by its use of high-resolution PRISM data in its process of climatically aided interpolation, where estimates of daily variables are placed in the context of long-term normals, reducing variance in spatial interpolation (Daly et al. 2008; Abatzoglou 2013). The differences in ETo estimates between NLDAS-2 and gridMET (gridMET generally estimates higher ETo in the lowlands, while NLDAS-2 estimates higher ETo in mountains) is likely due to gridMET’s explicit use of high resolution (4 km), topographically informed climatologies from PRISM. 

Correlations between input variables (Rn, Tmean, VPD, and wind) and ETo in the NLDAS-2 suggest that VPD is highly correlated with ETo residuals, while mean temperature and wind speed residuals are each moderately correlated (Figure 2C). Tmean error has a direct impact on VPD calculation, so its importance must be emphasized. In agricultural contexts, the large positive ETo estimation bias in NLDAS-2 is in agreement with the findings of previous studies (Lewis et al. 2014; Blankenau et al. 2020\) and is of considerable importance to overall water use given the high rates of ETo in the summer. The reduction in error of over 20% through bias correction suggests that NLDAS-2 can be used effectively if modified, though this error at the point of bias factor optimization may be assumed to be the most effective, with the correction losing efficacy with increased distance from the stations.

The higher relative accuracy of monthly estimates of ETa (RMSE \= 23.86 mm month\-1) compared to daily estimates during overpass (RMSE \= 1.16 mm day\-1) suggests some combination of random error cancellation, mitigation of outliers, smoothing of short-term random fluctuations, and improved signal-to-noise ratio. The moderate correlation of ETo estimates by NLDAS-2 at flux sites (r² \= 0.56), while biased, suggests that much of the error in daily estimates is derived from comparatively noisy SRS-based estimates of ETf (Figure  S6). This is supported by the results of the variance decomposition of the ETa data into ETo and ETf components, where a mean of 60% of error is attributed to the ETf component of the model.

   **5.0 Conclusion**

Our investigation of the use of NLDAS-2 for driving the SSEBop model to estimate ETa within the SMMW shows that NLDAS-2 data, while generally suitable for driving SSEBop, exhibits substantial random errors in daily estimates of meteorological inputs that appear seasonally nonrandom in nature in the study area. Despite a small overall bias in ETo estimates, errors are time-dependent, and lead to seasonal underestimates in winter and overestimates in summer, providing an opportunity to effectively correct the bias in the data at a monthly time scale. Generally, NLDAS-2 has similar performance across the U.S. and Canada international border, suggesting its appropriateness in international applications in North America. In comparison to gridMET, NLDAS-2 demonstrates comparable accuracy in overall ETo estimates, despite exhibiting larger errors in individual input variables. 

Our findings emphasize the importance of bias correction in NLDAS-2 data for agricultural applications. Monthly bias correction surfaces substantially improved the accuracy of ETo estimates, particularly during the critical summer growing season where uncorrected NLDAS-2 data tends to overestimate ETo. Our variance decomposition analysis indicates that VPD has the highest impact on ETo accuracy (68%), while wind speed and mean temperature errors also make substantial contributions (11% and 17%), and net radiation error plays a minor role (\~3%), underscoring the need for accurate modeling of humidity, wind, and temperature for reliable ETo estimation. The correlation between errors in different input variables demonstrates the importance of considering their combined effects in model development and evaluation.

The successful application of the bias-corrected NLDAS-2 data in the SSEBop model to estimate ETa at a high spatial and temporal resolution represents a significant step forward in transboundary water resource management. This study provides a framework for utilizing international datasets in areas with limited ground-based observations, enhancing our ability to quantify water consumption in agricultural systems and inform sustainable water management practices. Future research should focus on refining bias correction techniques, integrating additional data sources like remotely sensed soil moisture and land use, and exploring alternative modeling approaches. 

**Acknowledgements**  
Funding for this work was provided by the Water Availability and Use Science Program within the Water Mission Area of the U.S. Geological Survey. 

**Declaration of Interest**  
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

**Data Availability Statement**

The SSEBop ETa dataset, bias-correction surfaces, and processing code developed for this study are available via Sando et al. (2026). NLDAS-2 data are available from NASA GES DISC (https://disc.gsfc.nasa.gov). Landsat imagery is available from the USGS Earth Explorer (https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov). Weather station data were obtained from the sources cited in Section 2.2. Eddy covariance data from AmeriFlux sites are available at https://ameriflux.lbl.gov; ECCC flux data are available upon request from Environment and Climate Change Canada. The code used to perform this analysis is free and open-source, available at https://github.com/dgketchum/Milk\_SSEBop.

**References**

Abatzoglou, J. T. 2013\. "Development of gridded surface meteorological data for ecological applications and modelling." *International Journal of Climatology* 33 (1): 121–131.

Abatzoglou, J. T., S. Z. Dobrowski, S. A. Parks, and K. C. Hegewisch. 2018\. "TerraClimate, a high-resolution global dataset of monthly climate and climatic water balance from 1958–2015." *Scientific Data* 5 (1): 1–12.

Allen, R. 2008\. "Quality assessment of weather data and micrometeorological flux-impacts on evapotranspiration calculation." *Journal of Agricultural Meteorology* 64 (4): 191–204.

Allen, R. G. 1996\. "Assessing integrity of weather data for reference evapotranspiration estimation." *Journal of Irrigation and Drainage Engineering* 122 (2): 97–106.

Allen, R. G., C. E. Brockway, and J. L. Wright. 1983\. "Weather station siting and consumptive use estimates." *Journal of Water Resources Planning and Management* 109 (2): 134–136.

Allen, R. G., L. S. Pereira, D. Raes, and M. Smith. 1998\. *FAO irrigation and drainage paper No. 56\.* Rome: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations.

Allen, R. G., J. L. Wright, W. O. Pruitt, L. S. Pereira, and M. E. Jensen. 2007\. "Water requirements." In *Design and operation of farm irrigation systems,* 2nd ed., 208–288. American Society of Agricultural and Biological Engineers.

Blaney, H. F., and W. D. Criddle. 1962\. *Determining consumptive use and irrigation water requirements.* Technical Bulletin No. 1275\. United States Department of Agriculture.
